topic: A person finds out their partner has been learning their native language
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment had become a thick, suffocating blanket. I was scrolling through my phone, the screen illuminating the dust motes dancing in the afternoon sun, when I saw it. A notification from a language app my partner, Liam, had been quietly using. My stomach tightened, a familiar knot forming beneath my ribs. It wasn't a text; it was a progress report, a tiny, almost invisible marker showing his deepening fluency in my mother tongue. I slowly lowered the device, my fingers trembling slightly. When he walked in, whistling a tune I didn't recognize, I kept my gaze fixed on the floor. "Hey," he said, his voice bright, entirely oblivious to the landscape shifting within me. I managed a tight, shallow smile, the muscles in my jaw feeling permanently clenched. I just nodded, the effort of composure draining me dry.

[story 2]

I found the evidence tucked inside a book he’d left on the bedside table—a language conjugation chart, meticulously highlighted. The air in the room suddenly felt too thin, the sounds of the city outside seeming distant and irrelevant. Every breath I took felt labored, catching somewhere low in my chest. I didn't want to confront him; the thought of hearing his explanation, of seeing the pride in his eyes, felt like a physical assault. Instead, I started tidying, obsessively straightening the books on the shelf, my movements sharp and jerky. When he finally asked what was wrong, his tone was laced with concern, but I could only manage a clipped, "Nothing. Just tired." My hands shook as I placed a coaster down, the ceramic cool beneath my fingertips, a stark contrast to the internal heat rising within me.

[story 3]

It happened during dinner. Liam was recounting a story from his recent study session, his pronunciation surprisingly accurate, the cadence flowing effortlessly in the language I spoke best. I watched him, focusing intently on the way his mouth formed the unfamiliar sounds. A coldness seeped into my extremities, making my skin prickle. I picked at my food, chewing slowly, deliberately, trying to slow my own frantic heartbeat. When he looked up, expecting a reaction, I simply met his gaze, my eyes wide and strangely vacant. "That sounds… interesting," I murmured, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I pushed my plate away, the scrape of porcelain against the table echoing loudly in the sudden void between us. I needed air. I needed distance.
